1	# Commission Log — Standing Research Instrument
2	#
3	# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
4	# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
5	#
6	# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
7	# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
8	#
9	# Fields:
10	#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
11	#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
12	#   codex             — target codex name
13	#   complexity        — patron estimate at dispatch (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
14	#   complexity_post   — patron revised estimate after review, when dispatch
15	#                       estimate missed the actual footprint. Same scale as
16	#                       complexity. Fill only when the post-review judgment
17	#                       differs from dispatch; omit when they agree.
18	#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
19	#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
20	#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
21	#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
22	#   reviewed_at       — ISO date when patron reviewed this commission (null = not reviewed)
23	#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
24	#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
25	#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
26	#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
27	#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
28	#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
29	#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
30	#
31	# Scales:
32	#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
33	#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
34	#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned

# Commission Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per patron-sourced commission. Patron-subjective judgments only.
# Full artifacts live in experiments/data/commissions/<id>/ — see README there.
#
# IDs are writ IDs (w-*) from the Clerk. Pre-Clerk commissions used session
# IDs (ses-*) or ad-hoc labels — these are grandfathered with a note field.
#
# Fields:
#   id                — writ ID (canonical) or legacy session/label ID
#   title             — short description of the commissioned work
#   codex             — target codex name
#   complexity        — patron estimate at dispatch (Fibonacci: 1 2 3 5 8 13 21)
#   complexity_post   — patron revised estimate after review, when dispatch
#                       estimate missed the actual footprint. Same scale as
#                       complexity. Fill only when the post-review judgment
#                       differs from dispatch; omit when they agree.
#   spec_quality_pre  — patron assessment of spec before dispatch
#   outcome           — patron assessment after review
#   revision_required — whether follow-up work was needed
#   spec_quality_post — patron assessment of spec after review
#   reviewed_at       — ISO date when patron reviewed this commission (null = not reviewed)
#                       Backfill note: all commissions through w-mnjp1ph3 were reviewed by
#                       the patron (with Coco) on the dates shown. Dates are approximate,
#                       derived from review-related commit timestamps. After w-mnjp1ph3,
#                       patron review became ad hoc — the planning workshop and static
#                       implement→review→revise rig structure reduced perceived need.
#   failure_mode      — if outcome != success, what went wrong (omit if n/a)
#   note              — meta info about the record itself (not content)
#
# Scales:
#   Complexity (Fibonacci): 1 2 3 5 8 13 21
#   Spec quality: strong | adequate | weak
#   Outcome: success | partial | wrong | abandoned
#   Failure mode: spec_ambiguous | requirement_wrong | execution_error | complexity_overrun | broken | incomplete

commissions:
  - id: ses-93ad1c4c
    title: "Implement Clerk apparatus (MVP)"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: abandoned
    failure_mode: execution_error
    reviewed_at: 2026-04-02
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: C002
    title: "Implement Dispatch apparatus"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 3
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is ad-hoc label, not writ ID. Never dispatched — superseded by ses-0334962d."

  - id: ses-19194146
    title: "Implement Clerk apparatus (MVP) — re-dispatch"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 5
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: partial
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: adequate
    reviewed_at: 2026-04-02
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-0334962d
    title: "Implement Dispatch apparatus"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 3
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong
    reviewed_at: 2026-04-02
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-2149b518
    title: "Scriptorium seal/push sync hardening"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: success
    revision_required: false
    spec_quality_post: strong
    reviewed_at: 2026-04-02
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-053770d1
    title: "Clerk apparatus — align implementation with spec"
    codex: nexus
    complexity: 2
    spec_quality_pre: strong
    outcome: partial
    revision_required: true
    spec_quality_post: strong
    reviewed_at: 2026-04-02
    note: "Pre-Clerk: id is session ID, not writ ID"

  - id: ses-8cdbbc39
    title: "Dispatch apparatus — align implementation with spec"

1	# Vision authoring on disk: `nsg vision apply` CLI
2	
3	## Intent
4	
5	Add a file-based authoring surface for cartograph visions. The patron writes vision prose in `<GUILD>/vision/<slug>/vision.md` with a `vision-metadata.yml` sidecar, and runs `nsg vision apply <slug>` to snapshot the vision into the cartograph as a vision writ + VisionDoc. The CLI is the single code path for both first-time bootstrap and Nth re-import, identifying the bound writ via a `visionId` field written into the sidecar after first apply.
6	
7	This commission delivers Phase 1 of the surveying-cascade architecture (see `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`). It ships standalone — there is no surveyor-apparatus consuming the writes yet; the `ext['surveyor']` priority hints written by this commission are inert until a future commission lands the substrate.
8	
9	## Motivation
10	
11	Today, patrons author cartograph visions directly via `nsg vision create --title --body`, which works for one-shot creation but is awkward for prose-heavy long-lived visions and offers no way to re-import an edited vision without throwing away identity. The on-disk authoring flow gives patrons a normal file editor experience with persistent identity, the same flow for first/Nth import, and a natural place to attach priority hints.
12	
13	## Non-negotiable decisions
14	
15	### Sidecar carries the durable visionId binding
16	
17	After first apply, `vision-metadata.yml` carries a `visionId` field that binds the file tree to its cartograph writ. The slug is a directory affordance only; identity lives in the sidecar's `visionId`. The slug can be renamed; the binding survives. Source: c-mojeikyh.
18	
19	### Single code path for first-import and Nth-re-import
20	
21	`nsg vision apply <slug>` resolves the bound writ via `visionId` if present (re-import) or creates a new vision writ if absent (first import); the rest of the work is identical: copy `vision.md` content into `writ.body`, sync stage/codex from the sidecar, write priority hints into `ext['surveyor']`, and on first run write the new id back into the sidecar.
22	
23	### Single-event-per-apply guarantee
24	
25	A single `nsg vision apply` MUST produce a single Stacks CDC event on the cartograph book per logical operation, not separate create+transition events. This is a correctness requirement for downstream consumers that observe the cartograph book and react to vision changes. Two implementation paths satisfy this:
26	
27	- Extend `cartograph.createVision` to accept an initial stage parameter so creation lands at the desired stage in one transaction.
28	- Have the apply CLI wrap `createVision` + `transitionVision` in a single Stacks transaction.
29	
30	Either is acceptable. The implementer chooses. Source: surveying-cascade arch doc §3.6.
31	
32	### Priority hints flow through `ext['surveyor']`
33	
34	The CLI writes a `SurveyorExt` payload to the vision writ's `ext['surveyor']` slot, sourced from CLI flags merged over sidecar fields. The shape:
35	
36	```typescript
37	interface SurveyorExt {
38	  severity?: 'moderate' | 'serious' | 'critical';
39	  deadline?: string;       // ISO date
40	  decay?: boolean;
41	  complexity?: 'bounded' | 'unbounded';
42	}
43	```
44	
45	CLI flags: `--severity`, `--deadline`, `--decay`. Sidecar fields with the same names. CLI flags override sidecar values when both are present. Source: arch doc §3.10.
46	
47	The `ext['surveyor']` slot will be owned by the future surveyor-apparatus plugin (a separate commission). For v0 this commission writes to the slot before its owner exists; this is fine — `clerk.setWritExt(slotKey, ...)` is plugin-keyed and accepts any plugin id at write time. The future substrate will read these values when it lands.
48	
49	### Stale-binding handling

file.content:
# Vision authoring on disk: `nsg vision apply` CLI

## Intent

Add a file-based authoring surface for cartograph visions. The patron writes vision prose in `<GUILD>/vision/<slug>/vision.md` with a `vision-metadata.yml` sidecar, and runs `nsg vision apply <slug>` to snapshot the vision into the cartograph as a vision writ + VisionDoc. The CLI is the single code path for both first-time bootstrap and Nth re-import, identifying the bound writ via a `visionId` field written into the sidecar after first apply.

This commission delivers Phase 1 of the surveying-cascade architecture (see `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`). It ships standalone — there is no surveyor-apparatus consuming the writes yet; the `ext['surveyor']` priority hints written by this commission are inert until a future commission lands the substrate.

## Motivation

Today, patrons author cartograph visions directly via `nsg vision create --title --body`, which works for one-shot creation but is awkward for prose-heavy long-lived visions and offers no way to re-import an edited vision without throwing away identity. The on-disk authoring flow gives patrons a normal file editor experience with persistent identity, the same flow for first/Nth import, and a natural place to attach priority hints.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Sidecar carries the durable visionId binding

After first apply, `vision-metadata.yml` carries a `visionId` field that binds the file tree to its cartograph writ. The slug is a directory affordance only; identity lives in the sidecar's `visionId`. The slug can be renamed; the binding survives. Source: c-mojeikyh.

### Single code path for first-import and Nth-re-import

`nsg vision apply <slug>` resolves the bound writ via `visionId` if present (re-import) or creates a new vision writ if absent (first import); the rest of the work is identical: copy `vision.md` content into `writ.body`, sync stage/codex from the sidecar, write priority hints into `ext['surveyor']`, and on first run write the new id back into the sidecar.

### Single-event-per-apply guarantee

A single `nsg vision apply` MUST produce a single Stacks CDC event on the cartograph book per logical operation, not separate create+transition events. This is a correctness requirement for downstream consumers that observe the cartograph book and react to vision changes. Two implementation paths satisfy this:

- Extend `cartograph.createVision` to accept an initial stage parameter so creation lands at the desired stage in one transaction.
- Have the apply CLI wrap `createVision` + `transitionVision` in a single Stacks transaction.

Either is acceptable. The implementer chooses. Source: surveying-cascade arch doc §3.6.

### Priority hints flow through `ext['surveyor']`

The CLI writes a `SurveyorExt` payload to the vision writ's `ext['surveyor']` slot, sourced from CLI flags merged over sidecar fields. The shape:

```typescript
interface SurveyorExt {
  severity?: 'moderate' | 'serious' | 'critical';
  deadline?: string;       // ISO date
  decay?: boolean;
  complexity?: 'bounded' | 'unbounded';
}
```

CLI flags: `--severity`, `--deadline`, `--decay`. Sidecar fields with the same names. CLI flags override sidecar values when both are present. Source: arch doc §3.10.

The `ext['surveyor']` slot will be owned by the future surveyor-apparatus plugin (a separate commission). For v0 this commission writes to the slot before its owner exists; this is fine — `clerk.setWritExt(slotKey, ...)` is plugin-keyed and accepts any plugin id at write time. The future substrate will read these values when it lands.

### Stale-binding handling

input.command:
python3 -c "
import json
with open('experiments/data/patron-anima-gap/review-results.json') as f:
    data = json.load(f)
total = data['overallSummary']['decisions']
print('Writ count:', data['overallSummary']['writs'])
print('Decision count:', total)
print('Confirmed:', data['overallSummary']['confirmed'])
print('Dissent:', data['overallSummary']['dissent'])
print('Abstained:', data['overallSummary']['abstained'])

# Count overrides (animaSelection != primerSelected) and low confidence
overrides = 0
low_conf_in_confirmed = 0
total_confirmed = 0
for w in data['writs']:
    for d in w['decisions']:
        if d['animaSelection'] != d['primerSelected']:
            overrides += 1
        if d['animaVerdict'] == 'confirm':
            total_confirmed += 1
            if d.get('confidence') == 'low':
                low_conf_in_confirmed += 1

print()
print('Overrides (animaSelection != primerSelected):', overrides)
print('Total confirmed:', total_confirmed)
print('Confirmed with low confidence:', low_conf_in_confirmed)
print()
print(f'Override rate: {overrides/total*100:.1f}%')
print(f'Low-confidence rate among confirmed: {low_conf_in_confirmed/total_confirmed*100:.1f}%')
"

content.text:
Hoot — I want to flag something before I give you the numbers, Sean: the dataset doesn't quite match the question you asked.

## Dataset reality check

The only place we have planner-vs-patron-anima decision data is `experiments/data/patron-anima-gap/review-results.json` — and that snapshot covers **14 writs / 167 decisions** from the 2026-04-22 → 2026-04-23 window of the patron-anima-gap experiment, not the last 25 completed commissions on the commission log. We don't have an instrument running this kind of retrospective patron-anima review across arbitrary commission windows.

So I can either:
- (a) report numbers from those 14 writs and call it what it is, or
- (b) tell you "no data" if you actually wanted the most recent 25 commissions on the log.

Going with (a) and labelling it honestly:

## Numbers from the patron-anima-gap window (14 writs, 167 decisions)

| Metric | Count | % |
|---|---|---|
| Patron anima overrode planner selection (`animaSelection != primerSelected`) | 1 / 167 | **0.6%** |
| Confirmed decisions tagged low-confidence | 37 / 167 confirmed | **22.2%** |

A couple of caveats worth noting:

1. **The "override" framing is generous.** The overall summary records `dissent: 0, abstained: 0` for all 167 decisions — every one was `animaVerdict: confirm`. The single `animaSelection != primerSelected` case is one where the anima still confirmed but picked a different option than the primer's `selected` value (probably a primer/recommendation mismatch upstream rather than a real review override). True dissent rate is **0%**.
2. **All 167 decisions were confirmed**, so "% of confirmed decisions with low confidence" and "% of all decisions with low confidence" are the same number here — 22.2%.

## What I'd want before trusting this as a trend

- Is the question really about *this* window (X008-ish gap data), or do you want me to spin up a new patron-anima review pass over the actual last 25 commission-log entries? The latter would need a fresh review run — the data doesn't exist yet.
- Want me to dig into the 37 low-confidence confirms to see if there's a pattern (specific writs? specific question types?)?

lastPrompt: look at the last 25  completed commissions. what percentage of planner-proposed decisions did our patron anima override? what percentage of confirmed decisions were withow confidence?

content: Goal was stats on planner-vs-patron-anima decisions across the last 25 completed commissions. The available data only covers 14 writs from the patron-anima-gap window (0.6% override, 22.2% low-confidence). Next: confirm whether to run a fresh review pass over the actual 25. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
# c-mod66upj-5ab8d2fdced4 [live]
> Look at how much patron-anima overrides vs rubber-stamps in live rigs — is it paying its cost?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-24T17:14:03.895Z

## c-moe2dawd-d53ca4fb019d [concluded]
> Why did decision-review escalate ir-moe06752 to the patron when patron-anima had already pre-filled both decisions with the recommended option and ticked all scope items true?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: String-match mismatch in decision-review's confirm validator. Patron-anima emitted verdicts on all 24 decisions including D17 and D24, both confirm. But the prompt renders 'Primer recommendation' as a short label for long option keys (D17: 'Top-of-handler validation' vs the full option-key sentence) and with markdown backtick formatting (D24: backticks in prompt, plain in emission). Patron-anima followed its instruction to emit the full option key; validator compared against the short/formatted primer-rec string → no match → treated as untouched → escalated. Other low-conf confirms (D5/D8/D9/etc.) passed because their option-key and primer-rec strings were char-identical. Fix: either normalize on the validator (strip formatting, allow prefix match) or normalize on the prompt (always render primer-rec as full option key). Evidence: transcript jsonl 61daa07a in draft-modvzwcl-36d6ac10 shows patron-anima's full emission array; rig-modvzvb3 yields show touchedDecisionIds skipping exactly D17 and D24.
Created: 2026-04-25T08:14:52.525Z
Resolved: 2026-04-25T08:20:56.924Z

content.text:
Big claim, Sean — let me make sure I'm tracking your intent before I plant a click on it.

## What I think you're saying

Every plandoc should carry the four patron-anima review fields per decision, baked into the structure itself:
- `animaSelection` (what the patron-anima would pick)
- `animaVerdict` (`confirm` / `dissent` / `abstain`)
- `confidence` (`high` / `low`)
- `rationale`

So the override-rate and low-confidence-rate stop being retrospective audits over selected windows and become **standing readouts on every commission** — queryable any time, no special review pass needed.

That moves us from "we ran a patron-anima review batch on April 22-23" to "every plandoc, by definition, has a patron-anima signature on it." Which is a structural change to the plan, not just a measurement habit.

## Existing scaffolding I see in the tree

- **c-mo4bon6e** — designing the PlanDoc structure (live) ← natural parent
- **c-mo9hnid0** — monitor override rate / abstention rate across rigs (live)
- **c-mod66upj** — is patron-anima paying its cost vs rubber-stamping (live)
- **c-moe2dawd** — already concluded; patron-anima review is *already* running on real plans (it's how decision-review escalates), so the fields exist somewhere — the question may be whether they're durably attached to the plandoc artifact vs. emitted as a transient review yield.

So part of this might already be in motion — what you're claiming is that the fields shouldn't be a parallel artifact, they should live on the plandoc itself.

## Where I want a steer before parking the click

1. **Inline vs. attached.** Fields stamped onto each `Decision` block in the plandoc (one document, two columns of opinion), or a parallel `animaReview` block referenced by decision id?
2. **Synchronous dispatch gate, or just data?** Does dispatch pause when verdict = `dissent` / `abstain`, or does it only matter at review time?
3. **Scope.** All plandocs (every Astrolabe plan, including survey/scaffold/etc.), or just commission-bearing mandate plans?
4. **Cost.** Every plan now adds a patron-anima pass; are we OK with that as table stakes, or do we want to gate it (e.g., only writs above complexity X)?

If you confirm the read, I'll park this as a child of **c-mo4bon6e** (PlanDoc structure) with a cross-link to **c-mo9hnid0** (override-rate monitor) — those two are the natural homes.

content: no, I mean it already does. so if you look at the last 25 writs... then query the plandocs (which have the same id) the data is in there

Usage: nsg [options] [command]

Nexus Mk 2.1 — guild CLI

Options:
  --guild-root <path>                    Guild root directory (default: $GUILD_ROOT or auto-detect from cwd)
  -h, --help                             display help for command

Commands:
  init [options] [path]                  Create a new guild — directory structure, guild.json, and package.json
  version [options]                      Show Nexus framework and installed plugin version information
  status [options]                       Show guild identity and installed plugin summary
  upgrade [options]                      Upgrade the guild framework and run pending plugin migrations
  start [options]                        Start the guild daemon (tool server + oculus + spider crawl loop)
  stop [options]                         Stop the guild daemon (graceful SIGTERM with SIGKILL escalation)
  plugin                                 plugin commands
  signal [options] <name>                Emit a custom event into the Clockworks events book
  clock                                  Clockworks operator commands
  session                                session commands
  summon [options] [prompt]              Summon an anima — compose context and launch a session
  animator-status                        Show the Animator's current rate-limit pause state
  commission-post [options]              Post a new commission, creating a writ in open or new (draft) phase
  step-add [options] [mandateId]         Add a new step (atomic task) to a mandate for sequential execution
  writ                                   writ commands
  plan                                   plan commands
  inventory-write [options] [planId]     Write the codebase inventory for a plan
  scope-write [options] [planId]         Write or replace the scope items for a plan
  decisions-write [options] [planId]     Write or replace the decisions for a plan
  observations-write [options] [planId]  Write primer observations for a plan
  spec-write [options] [planId]          Write the generated specification for a plan
  codex                                  codex commands
  draft                                  draft commands
  pulse                                  pulse commands
  tools                                  tools commands
  loom                                   loom commands
  oculus                                 Start the Oculus web dashboard and keep it running
  conversation                           conversation commands
  click                                  click commands
  crawl                                  crawl commands
  rig                                    rig commands
  input                                  input commands
  engine-designs                         List all registered engine designs with contributing plugin info
  block-types                            List all registered block types with contributing plugin info
  help [command]                         display help for command

[
  {
    "id": "w-mojmj0rc-daec01ea159a",
    "type": "mandate",
    "phase": "completed",
    "title": "Cartograph: collapse companion docs into `writ.ext['cartograph']`",
    "body": "# Cartograph: collapse companion docs into `writ.ext['cartograph']`

## Intent

The Cartograph apparatus today maintains three companion books — `cartograph/visions`, `cartograph/charges`, `cartograph/pieces` — each holding a `{id, stage, codex, createdAt, updatedAt}` document keyed one-for-one to a writ id. This pattern predates the writ-level `ext` slot. Now that `writ.ext` exists as a sanctioned plugin-keyed metadata slot with a transactional read-modify-write API, those companion books carry pure metadata that can live directly on the writ row.

This commission collapses the three companion books into a single `writ.ext['cartograph'] = { stage, codex }` sub-slot. The cartograph's typed-API surface stays — every existing callable continues to work with the same arguments and return shapes — but the internals shed three Stacks books, three companion-doc shapes, and the dual-write transactional ceremony around them. `createdAt` / `updatedAt` were always duplicates of the writ's own timestamps; they go away.

## Motivation

- **The companion-doc pattern is the pre-`ext` workaround for plugin-owned writ metadata.** Now that `ext` exists as the sanctioned slot, a pure-metadata companion book is exactly what `ext` was added to replace.
- **The substrate work that depends on the cartograph (the surveyor-apparatus, see Commission C in the surveying-cascade design subtree) is best designed against the post-cleanup shape.** Building the substrate's CDC observer, completion-stamping logic, and rig routing on top of the current companion-doc pattern, then reshaping it later, is strictly more work than landing the cleanup first. The substrate's CDC observer becomes a writ-type-filtered subscription (a primitive Stacks already supports cleanly) rather than a per-companion-book subscription.
- **No external behavioral change is intended.** Every consumer (CLI tools, tests, the apply CLI shipped in Commission A) reads the same shape from the same API. The cleanup is internal.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### 1. `ext['cartograph']` is the canonical home for stage and codex

```typescript
interface CartographExt {
  stage: VisionStage | ChargeStage | PieceStage;  // type depends on the writ type
  codex?: string;
}
```

The plugin id is `cartograph`. The slot is plugin-keyed under the established convention; the cartograph apparatus is the only writer.

### 2. The typed-API surface stays stable

`CartographApi` keeps every method it has today:

- `createVision` / `createCharge` / `createPiece`
- `showVision` / `showCharge` / `showPiece`
- `listVisions` / `listCharges` / `listPieces`
- `patchVision` / `patchCharge` / `patchPiece`
- `transitionVision` / `transitionCharge` / `transitionPiece`

Argument shapes (`CreateVisionRequest`, `TransitionChargeRequest`, etc.) and return shapes (`VisionDoc`, `ChargeDoc`, `PieceDoc`) stay. The doc shapes are projections over `writ.ext['cartograph']` rather than rows in a separate book — but from the caller's view, nothing changes.

`createdAt` and `updatedAt` on the projection map to the writ's own timestamps.

### 3. Atomicity for create + transition

`clerk.transition` silently strips `ext` from its body (per the existing Clerk contract). Any operation that touches both `writ.phase` and `writ.ext['cartograph']` MUST wrap the `clerk.transition` and `clerk.setWritExt` calls in a single `stacks.transaction` so the pair is atomic. This applies to `createX` (post + setWritExt) and `transitionX` (transition + setWritExt) alike.

The existing `createVision({ stage })` parameter — which Commission A relied on for the single-event-per-apply guarantee — keeps its semantic: the cartograph writes the writ at its initial phase, stamps `ext['cartograph']` with the supplied stage, and (if the stage isn't the writ-type's initial state) transitions to it, all inside one `stacks.transaction`.

### 4. Read-side projection

`showVision(id)` / `listVisions(filters)` continue to return `VisionDoc`-shaped values. Internally they:

- read the writ via `clerk.show(id)` (or `clerk.list({ type: 'vision' })`)
- project `writ.ext['cartograph'].stage` and `writ.ext['cartograph'].codex` onto the returned shape
- carry the writ's `createdAt` and `updatedAt` directly

The existing `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` exported types stay as projection shapes (their definitions can be retained verbatim — the only difference is where the data comes from). Filtering on `stage` becomes a JSON-field filter against `writ.ext['cartograph'].stage`; Stacks' query layer supports this.

### 5. Companion-book deletion

`cartograph/visions`, `cartograph/charges`, and `cartograph/pieces` Stacks books are removed entirely. There is no migration of historical companion-doc rows — the cartograph is recently-shipped and nothing in production-style guild data depends on those books. Any test fixtures or integration tests that opened the companion books directly should be updated to read via the typed API.

### 6. Surveying-cascade arch doc updates

The settled architecture for the surveying cascade (`docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`) names the companion-doc pattern explicitly in three sections. Update them to reflect the post-cleanup shape:

- **§3.4 — Companion `SurveyDoc` holds envelope metadata only.** SurveyDoc was modelled on the cartograph companion-doc pattern. Rewrite this section so the survey-writ envelope metadata (rigVersion, surveyorId, completedAt — minus targetNodeId, which is already `writ.parentId`, and minus rigName, which is already `writ.type`) lives in `status['surveyor']` / `ext['surveyor']` sub-slots on the survey writ rather than in a `books.surveys` companion book. Note that `completedAt` is redundant with `writ.resolvedAt`. The substrate is the only writer.

- **§3.6 — Substrate watches cartograph CDC.** The current text lists three book-event streams (`book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.charges.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.pieces.{created,updated}`). Update to a single subscription on the writs book filtered by `writ.type ∈ {vision, charge, piece}`. The single-event-per-apply guarantee discussion stays — it shifts from "wrap createVision + transition in one transaction" to "the cartograph's createX/transitionX primitives are already transactional" with the same outcome (one CDC-significant phase transition per apply).

- **§3.7 — Substrate plugin shape.** The substrate-owned-things list includes "Owns survey writ types and `books.surveys`." Drop `books.surveys` from the list. The substrate now owns the `status['surveyor']` and `ext['surveyor']` slots on survey writs instead. Likewise drop "Stamps SurveyDoc on completion" and replace with "Stamps `status['surveyor']` on completion."

These three sections are the only load-bearing references; minor incidental references elsewhere in the doc may need to be touched up for consistency.

## Out of scope

- **Migrating SurveyDoc itself.** The surveyor-apparatus doesn't exist yet (Commission C in the surveying-cascade design subtree). This commission updates the arch doc to reflect the post-cleanup shape so Commission C is briefed against it, but does not build the substrate.
- **Migrating other companion-doc patterns elsewhere in the framework.** The Astrolabe `PlanDoc` and similar are out of scope; PlanDoc is mostly content (inventory, scope, decisions, spec) and isn't a metadata-only companion.
- **Reworking the `ext` API itself.** `clerk.setWritExt` and the slot-write contract are existing primitives; this commission consumes them, doesn't extend them.
- **Renaming `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` projection types.** They keep their names and shapes — only their backing storage moves.

## References

- Parent design subtree: `c-moji050w` — Cartograph + surveying cascade.
- Downstream consumer: `c-moji0ggh` — Commission C, the surveyor-apparatus substrate, which depends on this cleanup landing first.
- The `setWritExt` API contract is documented on `ClerkApi` in `clerk-apparatus`'s public types — that's the load-bearing primitive.",

    "body": "# Cleanup: delete vision-keeper plugin + redefine The Surveyor

## Intent

Two-part cleanup of the framework architecture in preparation for the surveying-cascade work:

1. Delete the `@shardworks/vision-keeper-apparatus` plugin entirely. It exists as a Reckoner-contract worked-example petitioner that submits drift snapshots; its semantics differ fundamentally from the cartograph-decomposition surveyor that the new architecture defines, and its presence creates a naming collision and conceptual landmine for anyone reading the codebase.

2. Update the framework architecture documentation to redefine "The Surveyor" as the cartograph-decomposition apparatus described in the new architecture document. The previous codex-mapping framing of Surveyor is superseded by the new meaning.

This is a sanctum-coordinated framework change with no implementation logic — purely deletions and doc updates.

## Motivation

The vision-keeper plugin was built as a worked example for the Reckoner contract document — a reference petitioner exercising every contract surface. With the cartograph + surveying-cascade architecture now settled (see `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`), the vision-keeper concept is being subsumed: surveying is what we now mean by the role formerly called "vision-keeper", and it operates on cartograph nodes (visions/charges/pieces), not on drift snapshots.

The legacy plugin's continued presence creates two problems:

- **Naming collision.** The architecture's "Surveyor" reservation now points at the cartograph-decomposition apparatus; the existing `vision-keeper` plugin name is a stale framing.
- **Conceptual landmine.** Anyone reading the codebase sees `vision-keeper` and assumes it's the apparatus described in the new arch doc — which it isn't.

The Reckoner contract document can stand without an in-tree worked example, or a fresh worked example can be authored later when there's a real consumer to model.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Delete the entire `vision-keeper-apparatus` plugin

Remove `packages/plugins/vision-keeper/` and all references. This includes:

- The plugin's source files, tests, and package metadata.
- Any `guild.json` entries that include `'vision-keeper'` in their plugins list (test fixtures, example guild configs).
- Any test fixtures or integration tests that depend on it.
- Any Clockworks standing orders referencing `vision-keeper-on-decline` or other relays the plugin contributed.

### Rewrite worked-example references in the petitioner-registration contract doc

`docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md` cites the vision-keeper plugin as the canonical worked example. After this commission:

- Remove or rewrite worked-example sections that depend on the deleted plugin.
- The contract document should stand on its abstract description of the contract without requiring an in-tree example. If a section loses its purpose with the example removed, delete it.

### Redefine The Surveyor in framework architecture docs

Update the following to describe The Surveyor as the cartograph-decomposition apparatus (per the new arch doc at `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`):

- `docs/guild-metaphor.md` — the section on The Surveyor (currently describes a codex-awareness apparatus). Replace with the cartograph-decomposition framing: surveys cartograph nodes, produces structural decompositions, registers as a kit-contributable surveyor with the surveyor-apparatus substrate.
- `docs/architecture/index.md` — the System at a Glance description of The Surveyor (currently describes "tracks what work applies to each registered codex"). Replace with the new meaning. The sentence at line 286 ("not yet extracted as standalone packages") should be updated or removed depending on whether the future surveyor-apparatus is now anticipated as a planned package.
- `docs/architecture/plugins.md` — any references to the codex-mapping surveyor concept. Replace with the cartograph-decomposition framing or remove if no longer relevant.

The substrate apparatus (surveyor-apparatus) doesn't yet exist as code — its package will land in a separate future commission. The doc updates here describe the role conceptually so the architecture docs stop pointing at the obsolete codex-mapping framing.

### No replacement worked example needed

The Reckoner contract document is the load-bearing reference for petitioners. After this commission, the contract doc no longer cites an in-tree example; it stands on its own. A fresh worked example can be added later if a real consumer benefits from one.

## Out of scope

- Any new functionality. This is purely cleanup.
- The new surveyor-apparatus substrate plugin. Separate commission.
- Updates to the `cartograph` plugin's `vision-keeper.md` placeholder file — that file is an unused stub; address it during the substrate commission.
- Any sanctum-side vocabulary registry updates — those are already in place.

## Behavioral cases

- After this commission lands, `pnpm -r build` and `pnpm -r test` succeed across the entire monorepo. No dangling references to the deleted plugin.
- `guild.json` files in test fixtures that previously included `'vision-keeper'` no longer do.
- Architecture docs no longer describe The Surveyor as a codex-awareness apparatus.
- The `petitioner-registration.md` contract doc reads coherently without the worked-example anchor.

## References

- Parent click: c-moji0d5n (Commission B).
- Arch doc: `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` (the load-bearing replacement framing for The Surveyor).

    "title": "Vision authoring on disk: `nsg vision apply` CLI",
    "body": "# Vision authoring on disk: `nsg vision apply` CLI

## Intent

Add a file-based authoring surface for cartograph visions. The patron writes vision prose in `<GUILD>/vision/<slug>/vision.md` with a `vision-metadata.yml` sidecar, and runs `nsg vision apply <slug>` to snapshot the vision into the cartograph as a vision writ + VisionDoc. The CLI is the single code path for both first-time bootstrap and Nth re-import, identifying the bound writ via a `visionId` field written into the sidecar after first apply.

This commission delivers Phase 1 of the surveying-cascade architecture (see `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`). It ships standalone — there is no surveyor-apparatus consuming the writes yet; the `ext['surveyor']` priority hints written by this commission are inert until a future commission lands the substrate.

## Motivation

Today, patrons author cartograph visions directly via `nsg vision create --title --body`, which works for one-shot creation but is awkward for prose-heavy long-lived visions and offers no way to re-import an edited vision without throwing away identity. The on-disk authoring flow gives patrons a normal file editor experience with persistent identity, the same flow for first/Nth import, and a natural place to attach priority hints.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Sidecar carries the durable visionId binding

After first apply, `vision-metadata.yml` carries a `visionId` field that binds the file tree to its cartograph writ. The slug is a directory affordance only; identity lives in the sidecar's `visionId`. The slug can be renamed; the binding survives. Source: c-mojeikyh.

### Single code path for first-import and Nth-re-import

`nsg vision apply <slug>` resolves the bound writ via `visionId` if present (re-import) or creates a new vision writ if absent (first import); the rest of the work is identical: copy `vision.md` content into `writ.body`, sync stage/codex from the sidecar, write priority hints into `ext['surveyor']`, and on first run write the new id back into the sidecar.

### Single-event-per-apply guarantee

A single `nsg vision apply` MUST produce a single Stacks CDC event on the cartograph book per logical operation, not separate create+transition events. This is a correctness requirement for downstream consumers that observe the cartograph book and react to vision changes. Two implementation paths satisfy this:

- Extend `cartograph.createVision` to accept an initial stage parameter so creation lands at the desired stage in one transaction.
- Have the apply CLI wrap `createVision` + `transitionVision` in a single Stacks transaction.

Either is acceptable. The implementer chooses. Source: surveying-cascade arch doc §3.6.

### Priority hints flow through `ext['surveyor']`

The CLI writes a `SurveyorExt` payload to the vision writ's `ext['surveyor']` slot, sourced from CLI flags merged over sidecar fields. The shape:

```typescript
interface SurveyorExt {
  severity?: 'moderate' | 'serious' | 'critical';
  deadline?: string;       // ISO date
  decay?: boolean;
  complexity?: 'bounded' | 'unbounded';
}
```

CLI flags: `--severity`, `--deadline`, `--decay`. Sidecar fields with the same names. CLI flags override sidecar values when both are present. Source: arch doc §3.10.

The `ext['surveyor']` slot will be owned by the future surveyor-apparatus plugin (a separate commission). For v0 this commission writes to the slot before its owner exists; this is fine — `clerk.setWritExt(slotKey, ...)` is plugin-keyed and accepts any plugin id at write time. The future substrate will read these values when it lands.

### Stale-binding handling

If the sidecar references a `visionId` that no longer exists or was cancelled: warn at scan time; error on next apply attempt. Patron must transition the writ to sunset/cancelled explicitly before re-applying with a fresh sidecar.

### Data flow is one-way: file → writ

The writ is a snapshot of patron intent at apply time. The file is the editable source. Edits to the writ via `nsg vision patch` or `transition` do NOT propagate back to the file. Editing the file and re-applying is the canonical update path.

## Out of scope

- The surveyor-apparatus substrate that reads `ext['surveyor']` and emits survey petitions. Future commission.
- Any auto-trigger of apply (fs-watcher, git-hook). Patron runs apply explicitly.
- Drift detection between the file and the writ. Future work.
- Vision walkthrough or completion CLI. Future commissions.
- Deletion of disk vision files. Out of scope; patron handles via filesystem.

## Behavioral cases

- First apply creates a new vision writ; sidecar gains `visionId`.
- Nth apply with same sidecar updates `writ.body` and stage/codex; sidecar unchanged.
- Apply with `--severity` flag overrides sidecar severity.
- Apply with no flags uses sidecar values, falls back to substrate defaults where sidecar is silent.
- Apply where the sidecar's `visionId` references a missing or terminal-cancelled writ errors out cleanly without partial writes.
- Apply produces exactly one Stacks CDC event on the cartograph book per logical operation.

## References

- Source clicks: c-mojeikyh (vision authoring on disk decision); parent click c-moji0bdf (Commission A).
- Arch doc: `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` (sections 3.1, 3.6, 3.10).",

  {
    "id": "w-mojdwk37-84a1937ecaf8",
    "type": "mandate",
    "phase": "completed",
    "title": "Deferred-petition staleness diagnostic",
    "body": "# Deferred-petition staleness diagnostic

## Intent

When the Reckoner defers a petition for a structural reason that won't self-clear without external action, the petition is **stalled**. This design specifies how staleness is detected, where it surfaces, and what the system does about it.

The motivating case: a `dependency_failed` deferral. The dependent writ has reached terminal-failure; the petition's writ is held in `new` indefinitely (recoverable) until the failed dep is recovered or the petition is withdrawn. Without a stalled signal, these accumulate invisibly.

## Scope (v0)

**In scope.** Detect petitions deferred for `dependency_failed`; expose the stalled condition as derived metadata in `writ.status['reckoner']`; CDC-listener-driven update of that snapshot off the Reckonings book.

**Out of scope.** Active intervention (auto-decline, priority nudging); operator-configurable thresholds; named Clockworks events for petition transitions; cycle detection in the Reckoner's hot path; staleness flagging for `dependency_pending`, `queue_depth` (a.k.a. `wip_cap`), `priority`, `time_hold`, `patron_policy`, or `other` defer reasons.

## Decisions

### 1. Surface — `writ.status['reckoner']` snapshot, derived via CDC handler

A live snapshot of the Reckoner's consideration state lives on the writ at `status['reckoner']`, written via Clerk's sanctioned `setWritStatus(writId, 'reckoner', value)` helper (transactional read-modify-write, sibling sub-slots preserved).

The Reckonings journal stays unchanged — it remains the authoritative decision history. Staleness is **derived** from the journal, not embedded in journal rows. A future Sentinel apparatus can subscribe to the same channel without modifying the journal contract.

#### Snapshot shape

```typescript
interface ReckonerStatus {
  /** Most recent decision the Reckoner made about this writ. */
  decision: 'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op';

  /** Populated iff decision === 'deferred'. */
  deferReason?: ReckoningDeferReason;

  /** Running counters across all deferrals on this writ. */
  deferCount?: number;
  firstDeferredAt?: string;  // ISO timestamp
  lastDeferredAt?: string;   // ISO timestamp

  /** Stalled flag — true iff threshold rule for the current deferReason fires. */
  stalled?: boolean;
  stalledReason?: 'dependency_failed';  // expandable; v0 only this
  stalledSince?: string;                 // ISO timestamp; first tick stalled

  /** Bookkeeping. */
  lastEvaluatedAt: string;  // ISO timestamp; matches the journal row's consideredAt
}
```

The block is rewritten in full on every relevant CDC event (the Reckoner handler is its only writer, so wholesale replacement is safe).

### 2. Thresholds — hardcoded per-defer-reason; v0 flags only `dependency_failed`

| `deferReason` | Stalled threshold | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| `dependency_failed` | 1 tick (immediate) | Dep won't recover without external action; no point waiting |
| `dependency_pending` | not flagged | Real dependencies can take days; staleness here is normal, not pathological |
| `queue_depth` | not flagged | Petition is being considered, just losing capacity contention; not stalled |
| `priority` | not flagged | Same logic as queue_depth |
| `time_hold` | not flagged | Petition is intentionally waiting on wall-clock; not stalled |
| `patron_policy` | not flagged | Operator-set hold; not pathological |
| `other` | not flagged | Unknown; conservative non-flagging |

Threshold metric is `defer_count`. ISO-timestamped fields (`first_deferred_at`, `last_deferred_at`) live in the snapshot for query/display, but the gate is the count.

Configurability is parked. If a real operator need surfaces, the same path the cadence-config click (c-moixb74x) walks applies here.

### 3. Action — pure observation

No auto-decline. No priority nudging. No state mutation on the petition or writ beyond the snapshot block.

The parent click already chose to keep `dependency_failed` petitions recoverable (writ stays in `new`); active intervention here would conflict with that. Staleness surfaces the problem; the operator (or, eventually, vision-keeper) decides what to do.

### 4. Ownership — Reckoner kit ships a CDC handler

The detection logic lives in a Reckoner-kit-contributed CDC handler subscribed to `book.reckoner.reckonings.created`. Critical path of the tick — score, transition, append journal row — stays narrow. Snapshot maintenance is cross-cutting, handled outside the critical loop.

#### Handler flow

```
on book.reckoner.reckonings.created:
  let row = event.entry as ReckoningDoc
  let writ = await clerk.getWrit(row.writId)
  let prior = writ.status?.reckoner as ReckonerStatus | undefined

  let next = computeNextStatus(prior, row)   // pure function

  await clerk.setWritStatus(row.writId, 'reckoner', next)
```

`computeNextStatus(prior, row)` is a pure function:

- Carries `firstDeferredAt` forward from `prior` (preserves first-seen-as-deferred timestamp across deferrals).
- Computes `stalled` per the threshold table.
- Sets `stalledSince` to `row.consideredAt` on the false → true transition; preserves it across continued-stalled rows; clears it (and `stalled`) on transition to a non-stalled state.
- Wholesale-replaces the snapshot.

The handler runs uniformly on every Reckonings row regardless of `outcome` — including `'no-op'` rows. The pure function handles the `no-op` case naturally: `deferCount` doesn't advance on `no-op`, so `stalled` doesn't recompute, but `lastEvaluatedAt` bumps forward to reflect that the Reckoner did look at the petition. No special-casing in the handler.

### 5. Lifecycle interaction — pure metadata

Staleness does not change petition state, writ phase, or journal contents. It is observation only.

A petition can transition out of stalled via:

- **Acceptance** — dep cleared, `deferReason` no longer applies. Snapshot updates with `decision: 'accepted'`, no stalled flag.
- **Decline / withdrawal** — petitioner gives up. Snapshot updates with `decision: 'declined'`.
- **Reason change** — failed dep recovered, deferReason flips from `dependency_failed` to `dependency_pending` (no longer stalled). Snapshot updates; `stalledSince` cleared.

No explicit `petition.fresh` event needed — consumers wanting "no longer stalled" derive it from the snapshot transition.

## Implementation surface

### What ships

This commission introduces the Reckoner-side observation pipeline: a `ReckonerStatus` type, a CDC handler subscribed to `book.reckoner.reckonings.created`, and the `computeNextStatus` pure function the handler uses to derive the snapshot. Defer-row emission and the `dependency_failed` / `dependency_pending` defer reasons are introduced by the in-flight dependency-aware-consideration commission and are out of scope here.

### Sequencing

This commission **depends on** the dependency-aware-consideration commission landing — without defer-row emission, there's nothing for the staleness handler to react to. Wire a `depends-on` link from this writ to that one.

### Behavioral cases the design depends on

- A `dependency_failed` defer row produces a snapshot with `decision: 'deferred'`, `deferReason: 'dependency_failed'`, `stalled: true`, `stalledSince` = the row's `consideredAt`.
- A second `dependency_failed` defer row on the same writ preserves the original `stalledSince` (does not advance it on continued staleness).
- A `dependency_pending` defer row produces a snapshot with `stalled: false` even when the prior snapshot had `stalled: true` — the false→stalled and stalled→false transitions both update the flag, and the latter clears `stalledSince`.
- A `'no-op'` row updates `lastEvaluatedAt` but does not advance `deferCount` or change `stalled`. The handler does not special-case `no-op`; the pure function handles it uniformly.
- An `accepted` row produces a snapshot with `decision: 'accepted'`, `stalled: false`, `stalledSince` cleared.
- Two CDC events arriving close together produce a coherent snapshot — Clerk's `setWritStatus` is transactional read-modify-write, and the handler writes the full snapshot per event.
- A petitioner-initiated withdrawal (direct `clerk.transition` to `cancelled`, bypassing the Reckoner) produces no Reckonings row, so the snapshot lags. Consumers cross-check `writ.phase` before trusting the snapshot — same convention as for any apparatus-owned status block. The Reckoner apparatus doc names this expectation.

## Open questions for follow-up commissions

1. **Initial snapshot population.** When a writ is first considered, is the prior snapshot

## Scope and blast radius

The cartograph plugin is structurally **isolated** in the monorepo: every reference to `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` / `cartograph.{visions,charges,pieces}` lives inside `packages/plugins/cartograph/`. No other plugin consumes the typed API or reads the companion books. Searches across `packages/plugins/**`, `packages/framework/**`, and `docs/**` confirm this — only the cartograph itself, its tests, its README, and the architecture docs reference these types.

**Affected files:**

| File | Role | Touch |
|---|---|---|
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.ts` | Core apparatus + CartographApi impl | rewrite createX/showX/listX/patchX/transitionX bodies |

| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-list.ts` … `piece-list.ts` | List tools (3) | unchanged at the API level |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-patch.ts` … `piece-patch.ts` | Patch tools (3) | depends on patch-surface decision (D2) |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-create.ts` / `charge-create.ts` / `piece-create.ts` | Create tools (3) | unchanged at the API level |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-transition.ts` / `charge-transition.ts` / `piece-transition.ts` | Transition tools (3) | unchanged at the API level |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/render.ts` | Show / list rendering helpers — uses `doc.stage`, `doc.codex`, `doc.createdAt` | unchanged callable signature; backing data shape change is transparent |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-apply.ts` | On-disk vision authoring CLI | calls `cartograph.createVision({ stage, phase })` and `clerk.setWritExt(id, 'surveyor', payload)` — neither call's surface changes; substantive change is that `cartograph.patchVision({ codex })` may need new behavior depending on D2 |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.test.ts` | Apparatus tests — watches `('cartograph','visions')` for CDC events at line 345 | rewrite CDC subscription to watch `('clerk','writs')` filtered by `type === 'vision'`; rewrite "no second create event" assertions for new event shape |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools.test.ts` | CLI-tool integration tests | mostly unaffected — operates through tool handlers; book-level assertions absent |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-apply.test.ts` | Vision-apply integration tests — watches `('cartograph','visions')` at line 162 | rewrite CDC subscription to writs book; rewrite create/update count assertions (lines ~241, 267, 357–456, 492–538, 664) |
| `packages/plugins/cartograph/README.md` | Public README — three sections describe companion books in detail (lines 19–22, 138–169, 194 patron-facing CLI table) | concurrent doc updates needed (rewrite the "Companion documents" / "Books" sections; tweak the CLI patch description) |
| `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` | **DOES NOT EXIST in this working tree** — see Pre-conditions below | brief asks for §3.4 / §3.6 / §3.7 updates against this file |

**Cross-cutting concerns:** none in code. Doc cross-cutting: the brief calls out the surveying-cascade arch doc.

## Key types and interfaces

### `WritDoc.ext` (clerk-owned slot, `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts:124–157`)

The destination slot. A plugin-keyed map (`Record<PluginId, unknown>`). The cartograph will write `writ.ext['cartograph']` with shape `{ stage }` (or `{ stage, codex }` per D1).

- **Slot semantics:** "metadata-shape data attached at writ creation or registration time, rather than the post-hoc observation that `status` records." The brief's stage/codex companion-doc fields fit this exactly — they're per-writ identity attributes, not observations.
- **Sanctioned write path:** `ClerkApi.setWritExt(writId, pluginId, value)` performs a transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot. **The full sub-slot value is replaced wholesale** — partial-field updates require read-merge-write. (This drives D1 and D2: storing codex alongside stage means every transition has to round-trip codex through the slot; storing only stage avoids the read.)
- **Strip-from-transition:** `clerk.transition()` strips `ext` from its body so transition + setWritExt must be wrapped in an outer `stacks.transaction(...)` to be atomic.
- **Strip-from-post:** `clerk.post()` does **not** accept `ext` in its request — `ext` is only writable via setWritExt. So createX must wrap clerk.post (or its byte-for-byte replica) and setWritExt in one transaction.

### `ClerkApi` methods used by cartograph

- `clerk.post()` (`clerk.ts:650`) — takes `{ type, title, body, codex?, parentId? }`. Already opens its own `stacks.transaction` when `parentId` is set; nested transactions flatten via `StacksCore.runTransaction()` (`stacks-core.ts:191`).
- `clerk.transition(id, to, fields?)` (`clerk.ts:1065`) — strips `ext`, `status`, `id`, `phase`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`, `resolvedAt`, `parentId` from the fields-bag.
- `clerk.setWritExt(writId, pluginId, value)` (`clerk.ts:1188`) — transactional read-modify-write; bumps `updatedAt`.
- `clerk.show(id)` — read by id.
- `clerk.list({ type, ...filters })` (`clerk.ts:749`) — filter by type, parentId, phase, codex.
- `clerk.edit({ id, title?, body?, type?, codex? })` — patches the writ row. Type/codex changes only allowed in `phase === 'new'`. Used by vision-apply for body sync.
- `clerk.getWritTypeConfig(name)` — registry lookup; cartograph already uses this.

### Stacks query layer

`WhereClause` accepts dot-notation field names — `validateFieldName` (`packages/plugins/stacks/src/query.ts:14–21`) allows `[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+`. The SQLite backend translates each dotted name into `json_extract(content, '$.<dotted>')` (`sqlite-backend.ts:47–53`). So filtering on `ext.cartograph.stage` is supported by the query language.

**Index constraint:** indexes are scoped per `(ownerId, book)`. Cartograph's supportKit contributes books owned by `cartograph`. The clerk-owned `writs` book carries indexes only on what `clerk/src/clerk.ts:1242` declares — currently `phase, type, createdAt, parentId, [phase, type], [phase, createdAt], [parentId, phase]`. **Cartograph cannot extend the clerk-owned book's indexes from its own kit contribution** (each kit's books are namespaced by contributing pluginId at `stacks.ts:73`). Consequence: filtering by `ext.cartograph.stage` is an unindexed JSON-extract scan on the writs book unless clerk extends its own schema. (See D5.)

### Cartograph internals (current shape — to be rewritten)

- `createVision/Charge/Piece` open `stacks.transaction`, call `txWritsBook.put(writ)` then `txXBook.put(doc)` — this byte-for-byte replicates `clerk.post`'s validation including parent existence, parent-not-terminal, codex inheritance, and id generation.
- `transitionX` open `stacks.transaction`, validate the transition against the registered config, call `txWritsBook.patch(id, ...)` then `txXBook.patch(id, { stage, updatedAt })`.
- `showX` reads `xBook.get(id)` directly. **Today's behavior:** if the typed API doc is missing, throws "not found" — even if the writ row exists. This is not the projection's job after the cleanup (a writ without `ext['cartograph']` is in an unexpected state; throwing is the right move there too, but the failure mode shifts).
- `listX` queries `xBook.find(...)` with a where-clause on `[stage, codex]` and `orderBy: ['createdAt', 'desc']`.
- `patchX` calls `xBook.patch(id, fields)` directly. **Drift bug today:** vision-patch's `--codex` updates only the companion doc's codex; `writ.codex` (which clerk-side queries filter on) is untouched. This will surface again under any solution that puts codex in the ext slot but not on the writ.

### Companion books — to be removed

Three books contributed by cartograph's `supportKit` (`cartograph.ts:683–685`):

```typescript
books: {
  visions: { indexes: ['stage', 'codex', 'createdAt'] },
  charges: { indexes: ['stage', 'codex', 'createdAt'] },
  pieces:  { indexes: ['stage', 'codex', 'createdAt'] },
}
```

Removing these from supportKit drops the contribution. **Stacks has no `dropBook` primitive** — `ensureBook` is idempotent-create only (`sqlite-backend.ts:304`). Existing on-disk SQLite databases will retain the `books_cartograph_visions` etc. tables as dead-but-not-dropped storage. Per the brief's "no migration of historical companion-doc rows" line, that is acceptable. Note for the artificer: same applies to test fixtures using `MemoryBackend.ensureBook(...)` (cartograph.test.ts:133–141, vision-apply.test.ts) — those `ensureBook` calls become no-ops or removed lines.

## Adjacent patterns

**Reckoner uses the same ext-slot pattern.** `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/reckoner.ts` writes `writ.ext['reckoner']` with a `ReckonerExt` shape carrying `source`, `priority`, etc. — see `RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID` constant (line 132). The reckoner uses a two-step non-atomic create+stamp (`clerk.post` then `clerk.setWritExt`), explicitly chosen as non-atomic by D7 in that commission. The cartograph's brief diverges: it explicitly requires atomicity (createX must commit writ + ext together). This is the relevant precedent for "where the slot lives" but not for "how to write it" — cartograph needs the wrap-in-transaction pattern.

**Astrolabe's `PlanDoc` is **not** a parallel.** PlanDoc is content-heavy (`inventory`, `scope`, `decisions`, `spec` markdown bodies) — explicitly out-of-scope for ext migration per the brief, and the inventory confirms it: PlanDoc fields (lines 14–70 of `astrolabe/src/types.ts`) are bulk content, not writ identity attributes.

**Surveyor ext slot.** `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-apply.ts:38` defines `SURVEYOR_PLUGIN_ID = 'surveyor'`. The vision-apply tool writes a priority-hint payload into `writ.ext['surveyor']` for the future surveyor-apparatus to consume. After this commission, vision-apply's call to `clerk.setWritExt(id, 'surveyor', payload)` will sit alongside the new `clerk.setWritExt(id, 'cartograph', { stage })` — same writ, sibling slots, no conflict (per `setWritExt` contract: each plugin owns its sub-slot, siblings preserved under concurrent writers).

**Filtering through writ.ext via dot-notation.** Stacks' tier3 conformance (`stacks/src/conformance/tier3-queries.ts:165 — "3.11 Dot-notation for nested fields"`) confirms `[['parent.id', '=', 'p1']]` works. So `[['ext.cartograph.stage', '=', 'active']]` is a valid where-clause — just unindexed, since the writs book's indexes don't cover it.

## Existing context

- `packages/plugins/cartograph/vision-keeper.md` — placeholder stub. The README explicitly says "vision-keeper.md is a placeholder stub; the agent runtime ships in a separate commission." Not relevant to this commission.
- `c-moji0bdf` (Commission A) — concluded; dispatched as `w-moji5a2z`. Shipped vision-apply with `createVision({ phase, stage })` for the single-event-per-apply guarantee. The post-cleanup createVision must preserve this initial-state-bootstrap parameter (the brief says so explicitly).
- `c-moji0d5n` (Commission B) — concluded; dispatched as `w-moji63xm`. Updated guild-metaphor.md, architecture/index.md, architecture/plugins.md to redefine The Surveyor as the cartograph-decomposition apparatus. **Did NOT create the surveying-cascade.md arch doc** — the commit `0164cf6` only touched `architecture/index.md` and `guild-metaphor.md`. See "Doc/code discrepancies" below.
- `c-moji0ggh` (Commission C — Surveyor substrate) — live; depends on this commission landing first. Its goal text already reflects post-cleanup framing ("status['surveyor']/ext['surveyor'] sub-slots (no companion book)"), so the surveyor commission's brief will be drafted against the post-cleanup shape.

## Pre-conditions and doc/code discrepancies

### **Critical: `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` is not in this working tree.**

The brief's non-negotiable decision #6 mandates updates to §3.4 / §3.6 / §3.7 of `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`. The file does not exist in the working directory. Confirmed via:

- The brief and Commission B's resolution both reference the file as if it exists; click `c-moji050w` calls out the absolute path `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` — that path is the sanctum-side repo, **not** this working tree

**Implication for the artificer:** the file must be authored from scratch as part of this commission, OR the artificer must escalate that the file is missing before brief-stated section updates are possible. ### Other doc/code observations (concurrent doc updates needed — surface in inventory, do not lift)

- `packages/plugins/cartograph/README.md:19–22` describes "Three companion books (`visions`, `charges`, `pieces`) keyed by the writ id, each holding a typed companion document with the per-type stage and audit fields." — must be rewritten.
- `packages/plugins/cartograph/README.md:138–169` ("Companion documents" / "Books" sections) — must be rewritten or removed.
- `packages/plugins/cartograph/README.md:98–102` — describes the createX atomic-tx invariant; the prose mentions "writes the writ row and the companion doc" — must be reworked to say "writes the writ row and stamps `ext['cartograph']`".

### Test rewrites required (concurrent — not observation candidates)

- `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-apply.test.ts:147–155, 162` — same MemoryBackend ensureBook + watch rewrites. Lines 241, 267, 357–456, 492–538, 664 contain `fix.cdc.create` and `fix.cdc.update` assertions tied to the cartograph visions book that will need re-tuning.

## Substrate-/library-level constraints relevant to the implementation

1. **Nested transactions flatten.** `StacksCore.runTransaction` (`stacks-core.ts:191`) detects `activeTx` and runs the inner fn inside the existing tx without a new boundary. So wrapping `clerk.transition` (which opens its own tx) and `clerk.setWritExt` (also its own tx) inside an outer `stacks.transaction` is the brief-prescribed atomic pattern, and it Just Works.
2. **CDC events coalesce per-row within one tx** (`stacks/src/cdc.ts:87–137` `coalesceEvents`). Writing the writ row + setting ext['cartograph'] in one tx coalesces to a single `create` event (with the final state including `ext`) for the writs row. Same for transition + setWritExt — coalesces to a single `update`.
3. **clerk.post does not accept an external transaction context.** This is why the current cartograph replicates clerk.post's validation logic inline. The brief acknowledges this as ongoing cost; the cleanup does not change it.
4. **VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc must keep their `[key: string]: unknown` index signature** (per `BookEntry` constraint historically — but post-cleanup they're projection types, not BookEntry rows, so the index signature is no longer needed for satisfying Stacks. Still, brief says "their definitions can be retained verbatim", which keeps the signature in place. Decision D6 below.)
5. **Filter ordering preserved.** `clerk.list({ type })` orders by `createdAt desc`; the writs book has a `createdAt` index. So listVisions etc. preserve the existing ordering by default.

input.command:
nsg writ list --phase completed --type mandate --limit 25 --format json 2>&1 | python3 -c "
import json, sys, subprocess

writs = json.load(sys.stdin)
ids = [w['id'] for w in writs]

total_decisions = 0
overrides = 0          # patron.verdict == 'dissent' OR patron.selection != recommendation
dissents = 0
abstains = 0
confirms = 0
low_conf_confirmed = 0
low_conf_total = 0
plans_with_no_patron = 0
plans_loaded = 0
plans_failed = []

for wid in ids:
    try:
        out = subprocess.check_output(['nsg', 'plan', 'show', wid], text=True, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
        plan = json.loads(out)
    except Exception as e:
        plans_failed.append(wid)
        continue
    plans_loaded += 1
    decisions = plan.get('decisions') or []
    if not decisions:
        continue
    has_patron = False
    for d in decisions:
        total_decisions += 1
        p = d.get('patron')
        if not p:
            continue
        has_patron = True
        verdict = p.get('verdict')
        sel = p.get('selection')
        conf = p.get('confidence')
        rec = d.get('recommendation')
        if verdict == 'confirm':
            confirms += 1
        elif verdict == 'dissent':
            dissents += 1
        elif verdict == 'abstain':
            abstains += 1
        if verdict == 'dissent' or (sel and rec and sel != rec):
            overrides += 1
        if conf == 'low':
            low_conf_total += 1
            if verdict == 'confirm':
                low_conf_confirmed += 1
    if not has_patron:
        plans_with_no_patron += 1

print('Plans loaded:', plans_loaded, '/', len(ids))
print('Plans with no patron field on any decision:', plans_with_no_patron)
print('Plans failed:', plans_failed)
print('Total decisions:', total_decisions)
print('  confirm:', confirms)
print('  dissent:', dissents)
print('  abstain:', abstains)
print('Overrides (dissent OR selection!=recommendation):', overrides)
print('Low-confidence (any verdict):', low_conf_total)
print('Low-confidence among confirmed:', low_conf_confirmed)
print()
if total_decisions:
    print(f'Override rate (of all decisions): {overrides/total_decisions*100:.1f}%')
if confirms:
    print(f'Low-confidence rate among confirmed: {low_conf_confirmed/confirms*100:.1f}%')
"

input.command:
nsg writ list --phase completed --type mandate --limit 25 --format json 2>&1 | python3 -c "
import json, sys, subprocess

writs = json.load(sys.stdin)
ids = [w['id'] for w in writs]

decisions_no_patron = 0
decisions_total = 0
override_examples = []
no_patron_examples = []

for wid in ids:
    out = subprocess.check_output(['nsg', 'plan', 'show', wid], text=True, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
    plan = json.loads(out)
    for d in plan.get('decisions') or []:
        decisions_total += 1
        p = d.get('patron')
        if not p:
            decisions_no_patron += 1
            no_patron_examples.append((wid, d['id']))
            continue
        rec = d.get('recommendation')
        sel = p.get('selection')
        if sel and rec and sel != rec:
            override_examples.append({
                'writ': wid,
                'decision': d['id'],
                'recommendation': rec,
                'patron_selection': sel,
                'verdict': p.get('verdict'),
                'confidence': p.get('confidence'),
                'rationale': (p.get('rationale') or '')[:150],
            })

print('Total decisions:', decisions_total)
print('Decisions with no patron field:', decisions_no_patron)
print('No-patron examples (first 5):', no_patron_examples[:5])
print()
print('Override count:', len(override_examples))
print()
for o in override_examples:
    print(f\"{o['writ']} {o['decision']}: rec={o['recommendation']!r} -> patron={o['patron_selection']!r} ({o['verdict']}, {o['confidence']})\")
    print(f'    {o[\"rationale\"]}')
"

input.command:
nsg writ list --phase completed --type mandate --limit 25 --format json 2>&1 | python3 -c "
import json, sys, subprocess
from collections import Counter

writs = json.load(sys.stdin)
ids = [w['id'] for w in writs]

verdicts = Counter()
confidences = Counter()
verdict_x_conf = Counter()

for wid in ids:
    out = subprocess.check_output(['nsg', 'plan', 'show', wid], text=True, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
    plan = json.loads(out)
    for d in plan.get('decisions') or []:
        p = d.get('patron') or {}
        v = p.get('verdict', '<missing>')
        c = p.get('confidence', '<missing>')
        verdicts[v] += 1
        confidences[c] += 1
        verdict_x_conf[(v, c)] += 1

print('Verdicts:', dict(verdicts))
print('Confidences:', dict(confidences))
print('Verdict x Confidence:')
for k, n in sorted(verdict_x_conf.items()):
    print(f'  {k}: {n}')
"

content.text:
Got it — querying the actual plandocs gives the real numbers. Apologies for the detour through the gap-experiment snapshot; that was the wrong dataset.

## Last 25 completed mandates — patron-anima review stats

**433 decisions across 25 plandocs.** Every decision carries the `patron` field; no missing data.

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| % of planner-proposed decisions the patron-anima overrode | **3.5%** (15 / 433) |
| % of confirmed decisions tagged low-confidence | **25.4%** (106 / 418) |

### Verdict × confidence breakdown

|  | high | med | low | total |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **confirm** | 292 | 20 | 106 | 418 |
| **override** | 14 | 1 | 0 | 15 |

A few things jump out:

- **Overrides are decisive, not hedged.** 14/15 are high-confidence; 0 are low-confidence. When the patron-anima overrides, it's not on the fence.
- **Low-confidence confirms are a quarter of the work.** 106 decisions where the patron-anima accepted the planner's pick but flagged uncertainty about it — that's a richer signal than the override rate. Worth treating as the "patron-anima paused but didn't object" pile.
- **Override rate sits inside your target band.** c-mo9hnid0 set 5–20% as the "healthy" band; 3.5% is just below. Right at the rubber-stamping edge but not inside it (and the overrides themselves look principled — recurring hits on #2 fail-loud, #18 don't-earn-structure, #38 prefer-delete).

### Texture of the overrides

Most cluster around two recurring patron-anima moves:

- **`#2` fail-loud over silent-fallback** — D8 in `mojdwk37`, D8 in `moiy8hkv`, D6 in `moix1zoj`. Planner kept reaching for graceful-degradation; patron-anima kept yanking it toward fail-loud.
- **`#18` don't earn structure speculatively** — D11 / D13 in `moix1zoj`, D14 in `mohuvn8h`, D19 in `mohuvn8h`, D13 in `mohuvpu2`. Planner over-built for hypothetical second consumers.

These two principles account for ~10 of 15 overrides — the patron-anima is doing real work on a small number of recurring planner blind-spots.

Want me to park a click logging this snapshot, or pull the same query for a different window (e.g., last 50, or per-week trend)?

import json, re
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

d = json.load(open('/tmp/low_conf_confirms.json'))

# Categorize by what KIND of question this is
def categorize(item):
    q = item['question'].lower()
    rat = item['rationale'].lower()
    
    # naming questions

    if any(k in q for k in ['index signature', 'enum', 'unified type', 'discriminat', 'optional field', 'export ', 'typescript']):
        return '11-type-system'
    if any(k in q for k in ['file', 'directory', 'package.json', 'package directory', 'source layout', 'colocate', 'co-locate', 'live in', 'split', 'where do the']):
        return '12-package-file-org'
    if any(k in q for k in ['initial phase', 'initial stage', 'extended createvision']):
        return '08-internal-data-shape'
    if any(k in q for k in ['dependency relationship', 's1', 's2']):
        return '13-other'
    return '13-other'

CATEGORY_LABELS = {
    '01-docs-wording': 'Docs / wording',
    '02-naming-identifiers': 'Naming / identifiers',
    '03-test-shape': 'Test shape',
    '04-log-error-text': 'Log / error text',
    '05-transaction-event-shape': 'Transaction / event shape',
    '06-migration-legacy': 'Migration / legacy data',
    '07-control-flow': 'Control flow / ordering',
    '08-internal-data-shape': 'Internal data shape',
    '09-cli-config-defaults': 'CLI / config / defaults',
    '10-validation': 'Validation',
    '11-type-system': 'Type system minutiae',
    '12-package-file-org': 'Package / file org',
    '13-other': 'Other',
}

by_cat = defaultdict(list)
for item in d:
    c = categorize(item)
    by_cat[c].append(item)

# Build markdown
def short_writ(t):
    return t[:55].rstrip() + ('…' if len(t) > 55 else '')

def short_q(q):
    q = q.replace('\n', ' ').strip()
    return q[:130].rstrip() + ('…' if len(q) > 130 else '')

def short_writ_id(wid):
    # Trim id like "w-mojmj0rc-daec01ea159a" -> "w-mojmj0rc"
    parts = wid.split('-')
    return '-'.join(parts[:2]) if len(parts) >= 2 else wid

lines = []
lines.append('# Low-confidence confirms — last 25 completed mandates')
lines.append('')
lines.append(f'Total: {len(d)} decisions across 25 plandocs.')
lines.append('')
lines.append('**Universal rationale pattern: "no principle speaks/fires" — confirming the primer.**')
lines.append('All 106 low-confidence confirms cite the same reason: the patron-anima review surface has no principled basis to override or endorse, so it confirms but flags low confidence.')
lines.append('')
lines.append('## Category breakdown')
lines.append('')
lines.append('| Category | Count |')
lines.append('|---|---:|')
for k in sorted(by_cat.keys()):
    lines.append(f'| {CATEGORY_LABELS[k]} | {len(by_cat[k])} |')
lines.append(f'| **Total** | **{len(d)}** |')
lines.append('')

for k in sorted(by_cat.keys()):
    label = CATEGORY_LABELS[k]
    items = by_cat[k]
    lines.append(f'## {label} ({len(items)})')
    lines.append('')
    lines.append('| Writ | Dec | Question | Selected |')
    lines.append('|---|---|---|---|')
    for it in items:
        lines.append(f"| `{short_writ_id(it['writ'])}` — {short_writ(it['writ_title'])} | {it['decision']} | {short_q(it['question'])} | `{it['recommendation']}` |")
    lines.append('')

with open('/workspace/nexus-mk2/.scratch/low-conf-confirms.md', 'w') as f:
    f.write('\n'.join(lines))

print('Wrote /workspace/nexus-mk2/.scratch/low-conf-confirms.md')
print('Lines:', len(lines))
PYEOF
